Post_Title: 🚨 No dataset? No problem.  In Machine Learning, the real work starts with data c...
Post_Body:
🚨 No dataset? No problem.

In Machine Learning, the real work starts with data collection and not all data comes from Kaggle or UCI.

So I learned how to build my own dataset from the web using Web Scraping.
Here’s what I worked on:

Web scraping with Python
Pagination handling (multiple pages)
Data cleaning and extraction
Converting data into structured DataFrames
Exporting data to CSV

🛠 Tools used: requests, BeautifulSoup, pandas libraries

Key methods I learned:

requests:
requests.get() → fetch webpage
response.status_code → check request success
response.text → get HTML content
headers (User-Agent) → avoid blocking

BeautifulSoup:
BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser") → parse HTML
find() → extract single element
find_all() → extract multiple elements
.text → get visible text
tag navigation (e.g., book.h3.a["title"]) → extract attributes

Now I don’t just use datasets I can create them from scratch.
That’s a real ML mindset shift.

Post_Title: Most of the data we work with isn’t clean spreadsheets it’s hidden inside web pa...
Post_Body:
Most of the data we work with isn’t clean spreadsheets it’s hidden inside web pages.

So I tried pulling it out myself.
I scraped a Wikipedia table of the Top 100 publicly traded companies using BeautifulSoup + Pandas and turned raw HTML into a structured dataset.

• Extracted and structured ~100 rows across 7 key features (revenue, growth, employees, etc.)
• Converted messy web data into an analysis-ready DataFrame to CSV
• Understood how critical data collection & cleaning is before any real analysis begins

This wasn’t just scraping, it changed how I see data.
Before dashboards and models, there’s always this step.

Post_Title: Recently, a client sent me a list of restaurants and a set of criteria.  The gui...
Post_Body:
Recently, a client sent me a list of restaurants and a set of criteria.

The guideline was clear: go through each one, find the information she needed, and organize everything into a structured spreadsheet.

Straightforward on paper. But with over 200 entries, the real work is in the consistency, making sure every row is treated the same way, every gap is handled the same way, from the first entry to the last.

The client got a clean and ready-to-use file. I don't always know what clients do with the data after that. That's fine, my job ends when they can open the file and get straight to work.

That's what I do.

If you have data that needs collecting or organizing, let's talk.

What's the most tedious data task you've had to deal with lately?

Post_Title: When you work on web scraping at scale, getting the data is only half the job. T...
Post_Body:
When you work on web scraping at scale, getting the data is only half the job. The real challenge is making sure the data is accurate, complete, and reliable.

For every scraping project, I recommend having a strong data QA process in place. This becomes even more important when you are collecting large volumes of data regularly and your team depends on that data for decisions.

Here is a simple way to validate scraped data quality:
1.     Check the expected schema first
 Make sure the number of rows and columns looks right before going deeper.

2.     Review missing values
 Look for empty cells or unexpected gaps in important columns.

3.     Use basic text statistics
 When extracting text, check length, min, max, mean, and other patterns to catch unusual results.

4.     Keep a reference for verification
 Save the source URL, image name, PDF page number, or row number so you can randomly sample and compare the scraped data with the original source.

5.     Log errors clearly
 If something fails, write it into a log file and mark the cell with values like “Failed” or “Error” so you can review it later.

6.     Use Pydantic for validation
 It helps enforce structure and catch bad data early.

The best part is that after this process, you do not need to scrape everything again. You can re-scrape only the failed records instead of the full dataset.

These are some of the methods I use, and I would love to hear what others are doing as well.
